poster_title: Agentic Context Engineering: Evolving Contexts for Self-Improving Language Models
authors: Qizheng Zhang, Changran Hu, Shubhangi Upasani, Boyuan Ma, Fenglu Hong, Vamsidhar Kamanuru, Jay Rainton, Chen Wu, Mengmeng Ji, Hanchen Li, Urmish Thakker, James Zou, Kunle Olukotun
paper_info: Accepted at International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2026)

content: Context adaptation is crucial for LLM applications like agents and compound AI systems.
Contexts allow rapid integration of new knowledge and sharing across models.
Long-context LLMs and context-efficient inference make context-based approaches practical.
Brevity bias: many prompt optimizers prioritize concise instructions.
Context collapse: iterative rewriting often degrades into less informative summaries.
Detailed, task-specific knowledge is essential for agents and knowledge-intensive applications.
ACE treats contexts as evolving playbooks for LLMs.
Incorporates generation, reflection, and curation modules.
Prevents context collapse with structured, incremental updates.
panels.figure_caption: The ACE Framework. Inspired by Dynamic Cheatsheet, ACE adopts an agentic architecture with generation, reflection, and curation modules.

content: Evaluated on agent performance and domain-specific benchmarks.
Used AppWorld benchmark for agent evaluation.
Focused on financial analysis for domain-specific tasks.
panels.figure_caption: Example ACE-Generated Context on the AppWorld Benchmark (partially shown).

content: ACE outperformed strong baselines with average gains of 10.6% on agents and 8.6% on domain-specific benchmarks.
Matched the top-ranked production-level agent IBM-CUGA and surpassed it on the harder test-challenge split.
Achieved 86.9% lower adaptation latency and significantly fewer rollouts and lower costs.
panels.figure_caption: The AppWorld leaderboard as accessed on 09/20/2025.

content: Comprehensive, evolving contexts enable scalable, efficient, and self-improving LLM systems.
ACE provides a robust framework for context adaptation, outperforming existing methods in agent and domain-specific reasoning tasks.
